model_narrative: We run a cutting operation that slices wide raw rolls of stock into narrower finished rolls of various widths to meet customer orders. A fixed collection of cutting patterns is available, where each pattern is one recipe for how a single raw roll gets divided into finished pieces. For each pattern we decide how many raw rolls to cut using that pattern, and these counts must be whole numbers. The objective is to minimize the total number of raw rolls used across all patterns.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the set of finished widths that must be produced; each member is one ordered product width

sets.name: p
sets.doc: the set of available cutting patterns; each pattern is a fixed recipe describing how one raw roll is sliced into finished pieces

params.name: r
params.doc: the width of a single raw roll of stock material, in width units; a scalar equal to 100

params.name: w
params.doc: the width of each finished product, in width units

params.name: d
params.doc: the number of finished rolls of each width that must be delivered to satisfy demand

params.name: aip
params.doc: the number of finished pieces of a given width obtained from one application of a given pattern; zero when a pattern produces none of that width

vars.name: xp
vars.doc: the number of raw rolls cut according to each pattern; a whole-number count bounded below by zero and above by total demand

constraints.name: aip[i,
constraints.intent: Production must cover every order. For each finished width, the total number of finished pieces of that width produced across all the patterns we run must be at least the number of rolls of that width that customers demand. The pieces of a given width produced by a pattern depend on how many raw rolls we cut with that pattern and on how many pieces of that width the pattern yields per roll.

query: The two mixed-width patterns — p5 and p6 — both run on the same combination press, and maintenance is pushing back on how many cycles that line takes. There's a proposal on the table to hold the combined runs across those two patterns to no more than 230 rolls a cycle and let the single-width patterns pick up the slack. What total roll count does the optimizer return once that ceiling is in place?

query: Both pattern p3 and pattern p6 cut the 31-wide, but p3 gives a clean three-up while p6 only yields two as a side product. Engineering is pitching a sourcing rule that keeps the dedicated p3 runs at no less than half of however many p6 runs we schedule, so the 31-wide doesn't end up coming mostly off the by-product route. Plug that in and tell me where total roll usage lands.

query: Scheduling wants to avoid betting the whole cycle on any single knife setup, so nothing gets so dominant that one breakdown sinks the plan. We're looking at a blanket rule that no pattern, whichever one it is, runs more than 180 rolls in a cycle. Walk me through what that does to the total roll count.

query: It bugs me that patterns p2 and p3 — the clean single-width cuts for the 36-wide and the 31-wide — sit almost completely idle while everything funnels through the mixed patterns. Between the two of them, shouldn't they be carrying a real chunk of the load, say at least 300 rolls combined? What's the model seeing that keeps those two dedicated patterns parked?

query: Both p3 and p6 produce the 31-wide, but p3 cuts three clean per roll where p6 only throws off two on the side, and yet the plan leans entirely on p6 for it. Wouldn't it be obvious to run the dedicated p3 pattern at least as many times as p6 rather than the other way round? What's pulling the optimizer toward the by-product route instead?

query: The plan is dangerously lopsided — a couple of patterns run nearly 200 rolls each while most sit cold. From a resilience standpoint that's a lot of exposure on a single knife setup. Couldn't we just hold every pattern to no more than 150 rolls a cycle so the work is spread thinner across the board? What's the tradeoff the model is making by concentrating it the way it does?

query: Operations wants to trim back the big combination-press run on pattern p5 to about 150 rolls, and at the same time keep pattern p2 modest — no more than 30 rolls — since its changeover is a pain. On their own each of those caps looks harmless, but I suspect together they box the plan in. Couldn't we hold p5 down to 150 and p2 down to 30 at the same time without much damage? What's the interaction the model is reacting to that I'm not seeing?
